skill.name: spawn-parallel
skill.description: Guides AI assistants to split independent work into parallel subagent tasks with clear context, ownership boundaries, and result collection.
skill.summary: Coordinate independent subagent work in parallel while reducing file conflicts.
skill.author: Clouder0

security_audit.summary: The static external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by Markdown examples and ordinary prose. The skill contains instructions for Task-based subagent coordination, not executable scripts, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection. The main residual risk is operational: users must set clear file ownership when delegating parallel work.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code examples for Task prompts and JSON context. They are not Ruby or shell execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced Markdown examples or Task call templates. No executable script file, interpreter directive, or shell command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are triggered by normal prose and placeholder text. No cryptographic algorithm or security primitive is implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines describe the skill, show a JSON template, or include an original task placeholder. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or comparable weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance False Positive
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding points to retry logic for unclear agent output. It does not instruct the agent to collect host, user, network, or system inventory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a failure-handling decision tree about unclear task output. There is no evidence of system enumeration or data collection.

low_findings.title: Parallel Agent File Ownership Requires Care
low_findings.description: The skill delegates independent work to multiple agents and relies on boundaries to prevent file conflicts. Misconfigured ownership could cause conflicting edits, but this is an operational risk rather than malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly addresses file ownership and read-only resources. The risk depends on user prompts and agent execution context, so confidence is moderate.

content.value_statement: Large agent tasks often slow down when independent work is handled one step at a time. This skill shows how to divide safe parallel tasks, assign context, prevent conflicts, and collect results.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when tasks are independent enough for parallel delegation.
Defines focused context packets with owned files, read-only files, and output paths.
Shows how to launch multiple Task calls in one assistant response.
Provides recovery patterns for failed or unclear subagent results.
Demonstrates explorer, implementer, verifier, and tester subagent roles.
limitations: It is an instruction pattern, not an executable orchestration framework.
It assumes the environment supports the Task tool and custom subagent types.
It does not automatically enforce file ownership boundaries.
It does not merge outputs or resolve conflicts without user or agent follow-up.

use_cases.title: Split codebase exploration
use_cases.description: Assign separate explorers to authentication, API routes, and database models, then combine their findings.

use_cases.title: Parallelize independent implementation work
use_cases.description: Delegate unrelated changes to separate implementers with explicit file ownership and shared read-only context.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering teams using AI coding agents

use_cases.title: Coordinate verification tasks
use_cases.description: Run testing, review, and documentation checks as independent tasks before collecting results.

prompt_templates.title: Find independent tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this work request and list which tasks can run independently. For each task, name owned files, read-only files, and expected output.

prompt_templates.title: Create parallel agent prompts
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these independent tasks into parallel subagent prompts. Keep context minimal, define file boundaries, and require each agent to return a concise result.

prompt_templates.title: Recover failed subagent work
prompt_templates.prompt: Some parallel agents failed or returned unclear output. Classify each failure, suggest retry context, and identify any task that needs scope reduction.

prompt_templates.title: Design a full parallel workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a parallel agent workflow for this feature. Include task boundaries, shared contracts, verification steps, result collection, and conflict handling.

output_examples.input: Explore a new service layer and summarize risky areas quickly.
output: Three explorer agents are assigned to authentication, API routing, and data models.
Each agent receives only relevant files and returns a compact findings summary.
Results are collected after all agents finish, then reviewed for overlap.

output_examples.input: Implement validation and email service changes at the same time.
output: One implementer owns validator files while another owns email service files.
Both agents can read shared type and configuration files.
A verifier checks boundaries, tests, and integration assumptions after completion.

best_practices: Use parallel agents only when tasks have no output dependency on each other.
Give every agent explicit owned files, read-only files, and a required result format.
Validate each result before merging or starting dependent follow-up work.
anti_patterns: Do not assign the same writable file to multiple agents without a conflict plan.
Do not spawn agents with broad repository context when a focused file list is enough.
Do not continue retrying failed tasks without adding context or reducing scope.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions for coordinating Task-based subagents and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but the examples assume a Task tool is available.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when tasks are independent, can complete in any order, and do not need to write the same files.

faq.question: How does it prevent conflicts?
faq.answer: It asks the user or agent to define file ownership, read-only resources, and contracts before spawning subagents.

faq.question: Can it handle failures?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes retry, context enhancement, scope reduction, escalation, and abort paths for failed subagent work.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found false positive static findings and no evidence of malicious behavior.
